author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
The Tenth Item in the Accusation. The More Prominent Points of Cœlestius’ Work Continued.
title: The Tenth Item in the Accusation. The More Prominent Points of Cœlestius’ Work Continued.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Chapter 30 [XIV.]—The Tenth Item in the Accusation.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: The More Prominent Points of Cœlestius’ Work Continued.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: After this we find objected against Pelagius some other points of Cœlestius’ teaching,—prominent ones, and undoubtedly worthy of condemnation; such, indeed, as would certainly have involved Pelagius in condemnation, if he had not anathematized them in the synod.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Under his third head Cœlestius was alleged to have written: “That God’s grace and assistance is not given for single actions, but is imparted in the freedom of the will, or in the law and in doctrine.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: And again: “That God’s grace is given in proportion to our deserts; because, were He to give it to sinful persons, He would seem to be unrighteous.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: And from these words he inferred that “therefore grace itself has been placed in my will, according as I have been either worthy or unworthy of it.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: For if we do all things by grace, then whenever we are overcome by sin, it is not we who are overcome, but God’s grace, which wanted by all means to help us, but was not able.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: And once more he says: “If, when we conquer sin, it is by the grace of God; then it is He who is in fault whenever we are conquered by sin, because He was either altogether unable or unwilling to keep us safe.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: To these charges Pelagius replied: “Whether these are really the opinions of Cœlestius or not, is the concern of those who say that they are.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: For my own part, indeed, I never entertained such views; on the contrary, I anathematize every one who does entertain them.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Then the synod said: “This holy synod accepts you for your condemnation of these impious words.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Now certainly there can be no mistake, in regard to these opinions, either as to the clear way in which Pelagius pronounced on them his anathema, or as to the absolute terms in which the bishops condemned them.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Whether Pelagius or Cœlestius, or both of them, or neither of them, or other persons with them or in their name, have ever held or still hold these sentiments,—may be doubtful or obscure; but nevertheless by this judgment of the bishops it has been declared plainly enough that they have been condemned, and that Pelagius would have been condemned along with them, unless he had himself condemned them too.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Now, after this trial, it is certain that whenever we enter on a controversy touching opinions of this kind, we only discuss an already condemned heresy.
